result.markdown:
--- title: "[Surv]{.doc-object-name .doc-class .doc-label .doc-label-dataclass}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} A validated time-to-event response for survival analysis. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L117-L1171){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.CLASS} ```python Surv(type, stop, status, start=None, lower=None, states=None, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} `Surv` represents the outcome in survival models: a time at which each subject either experienced an event (observed) or was censored (did not experience the event during follow-up). `Surv` supports multiple censoring types: - **Right-censored** (most common): The event time is at or after the recorded time. Use `Surv.right(time, event)`. - **Left-censored**: The event time is before the recorded time. Use `Surv.left(time, event)`. - **Counting-process** (left truncation, time-varying): Each subject enters the risk set at `start` and exits at `stop`. Use `Surv.counting(start, stop, event)`. - **Interval-censored**: The event occurred within a time interval `[lower, upper)`. Use `Surv.interval(lower, upper)`. - **Multi-state / competing risks**: Multiple mutually exclusive events. Use `Surv.multistate(time, event, states)`. **Use the class methods** (`right`, `left`, `counting`, `interval`, `multistate`) to construct `Surv` objects. They validate your input and set the censoring type appropriately. As such, direct instantiation is not recommended. ::: ## Attributes {.doc-attributes} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [type]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [CensoringType]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The `CensoringType` enum indicating the censoring mechanism. [stop]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Exit time (for interval censoring, the upper bound). [status]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Integer event code per observation: 0 = censored, 1+ = event code (for multi-state, codes >= 1 index into `states`). [start]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Entry time for the counting-process form (left truncation) (`None` otherwise). [lower]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Lower bound for interval censoring (`None` otherwise). [states]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [tuple[str, ...] | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Event-state labels for multi-state/competing-risks endpoints (`None` for the single-event case). [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Optional case weights (strictly positive) (`None` if no weights provided). ::: ## Examples {.doc-examples} Here's an example of direct instantiation of `Surv`: ::: {#bda0b608 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw import numpy as np # Build a right-censored response via direct instantiation y = gw.Surv( type=gw.CensoringType.RIGHT, stop=np.array([5, 6, 4, 9]), status=np.array([1, 0, 1, 0]) ) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Surv(type=right, n=4, events=2) ``` ::: ::: While this is fine, the preferred approach is to use the class method constructors for each censoring type. They handle validation and conversion automatically. Right-censored (the most common case): each subject has an exit time and an event indicator. ::: {#501efb8c .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Build a right-censored response y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` Surv(type=right, n=4, events=2) ``` ::: ::: Counting-process form with left truncation (late entry): ::: {#0a1127fc .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Build a counting-process response with late entry y = gw.Surv.counting(start=[0, 2, 1], stop=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` Surv(type=counting, n=3, events=2, truncated) ``` ::: ::: Interval-censored (event known to occur in a time window): ::: {#244b7010 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Build an interval-censored response y = gw.Surv.interval(lower=[1, 3], upper=[3, 8]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` Surv(type=interval, n=2, events=2) ``` ::: ::: Multi-state (competing risks, multiple mutually exclusive events): ::: {#0a546db9 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Build a multi-state competing-risks response y = gw.Surv.multistate(time=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 2], states=("pcm", "death")) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` Surv(type=right, n=3, events=2, states=('pcm', 'death')) ``` ::: ::: ## Attributes {.doc-attributes}
Name Description
entry Entry times for each observation (or $-\infty$ if no left truncation).
event Boolean event indicator: True if any event occurred, False if censored.
is_multistate Whether the response has multiple competing event states.
is_truncated Whether the response has left truncation (late entry).
n Number of observations in the response.
n_censored Count of censored observations.
n_events Count of observations where an event occurred (any state in multi-state data).
### [entry]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Entry times for each observation (or $-\infty$ if no left truncation). ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [entry]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} For counting-process data (late entry), this returns the `start` time when each subject became at risk. For standard right-censored data with no left truncation, all values are $-\infty$, indicating subjects entered at the beginning of follow-up. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Right-censored data (no left truncation) has all $-\infty$ entry times: ::: {#efb0e17e .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y_right = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) # Entry times are -inf when there is no left truncation y_right.entry ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` array([-inf, -inf, -inf]) ``` ::: ::: Counting-process data shows each subject's entry time: ::: {#824d5865 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y_counting = gw.Surv.counting(start=[0, 2, 1], stop=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) # Entry times reflect each subject's actual start y_counting.entry ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` array([0., 2., 1.]) ``` ::: ::: The `entry` property is primarily used internally by survival estimators to correctly compute risk sets. You rarely need it directly, but it's available for custom analyses. ### [event]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Boolean event indicator: True if any event occurred, False if censored. ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [event]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Array]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Converts the integer `status` codes to a simple boolean: `1` or more -> True (event), `0` -> False (censored). This is a convenient summary when you only care about event occurrence, not which specific state occurred in multi-state data. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} ::: {#fcf381d2 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Boolean indicator: True where any event occurred y.event ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ``` array([ True, False, True, False]) ``` ::: ::: The `True`/`False` values indicate which subjects experienced any event. This is useful for filtering, counting events, or checking data quality. For multi-state data, this collapses all states into a single "any event" indicator: ::: {#3cd5a92d .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y_multi = gw.Surv.multistate( time=[5, 6, 7, 8], event=[1, 2, 0, 1], states=("relapse", "death") ) # Collapses all competing causes into a single indicator y_multi.event ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ``` array([ True, True, False, True]) ``` ::: ::: ### [is_multistate]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Whether the response has multiple competing event states. ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [is_multistate]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [bool]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Multi-state responses track which of several competing outcomes occurred (e.g., "relapse" vs. "death"). When False, there is only one event type (censored or not). When True, the `states` property contains the outcome labels. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Right-censored data has a single outcome: ::: {#45002e7d .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Single-event data is not multi-state y_right = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y_right.is_multistate ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` False ``` ::: ::: Multi-state data with competing risks: ::: {#b633d622 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Competing-risks data is multi-state y_multi = gw.Surv.multistate( time=[5, 6, 7, 8], event=[1, 2, 0, 1], states=("relapse", "death") ) y_multi.is_multistate ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` True ``` ::: ::: This property is useful for determining how to interpret the event codes and what kind of survival estimation is needed. ### [is_truncated]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Whether the response has left truncation (late entry). ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [is_truncated]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [bool]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Left truncation occurs in counting-process data when subjects enter the risk set at different times (late entry). This is common in studies with age-based entry or complex follow-up patterns. When True, the `entry()` property contains the actual start times. When False, all subjects implicitly start at time 0. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Right-censored data has no left truncation: ::: {#72e93263 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Right-censored data has no left truncation y_right = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y_right.is_truncated ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` False ``` ::: ::: Counting-process data with late entry is truncated: ::: {#9cc93c38 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Counting-process data with late entry is truncated y_counting = gw.Surv.counting(start=[0, 2, 1], stop=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y_counting.is_truncated ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ``` True ``` ::: ::: This property is useful for understanding data structure and for conditional logic that handles truncated vs. non-truncated data differently. ### [n]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Number of observations in the response. ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [n]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns the total count of subjects/observations, regardless of event status. Equivalent to `len(surv_object)`. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} ::: {#4867b0e1 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Get the number of observations y.n ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ``` 4 ``` ::: ::: This is useful for loops, validation, or allocating arrays. Often used to determine sample size or for sanity checks on data shape. ### [n_censored]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Count of censored observations. ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [n_censored]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Counts all observations where the event was not observed (status == 0). These are subjects whose true event time is unknown but exceeds their observation time. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} ::: {#35523842 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Count of censored observations y.n_censored ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=15} ``` 2 ``` ::: ::: Often used for descriptive summary: "We observed 2 events and 2 censored subjects out of 4 total." Can validate data quality: ::: {#725e5902 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Verify that events plus censorings equal the total assert y.n_events + y.n_censored == y.n ``` ::: Higher censoring rates reduce the information available for estimation and may require larger sample sizes for stable inference. ### [n_events]{.doc-object-name .doc-attribute .doc-label .doc-label-property} ::: {.doc-subject} Count of observations where an event occurred (any state in multi-state data). ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-attribute} [n_events]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int]{.doc-parameter-annotation} ::: ::: {.doc-text} Counts all observations with `status >= 1`. For multi-state responses, this counts all events regardless of which specific state occurred. For single-event data, this is the count of subjects who experienced the event. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} ::: {#e07a4230 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Count of observed events y.n_events ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=17} ``` 2 ``` ::: ::: This is useful for descriptive statistics, event rate calculations, or validating data: `assert y.n_events + y.n_censored == y.n`. For multi-state data, this gives the total event count across all states: ::: {#d08ddb21 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} y_multi = gw.Surv.multistate( time=[5, 6, 7, 8], event=[1, 2, 0, 1], states=("relapse", "death") ) # Total events across all competing causes y_multi.n_events ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=18} ``` 3 ``` ::: ::: ## Methods {.doc-methods}
Name Description
counting() Counting-process response: track subjects entering and exiting the risk set at
from_dict() Rebuild a response from `to_dict` output.
from_json() Deserialize from `to_json` output.
interval() Interval-censored response: event time is known to lie within a range.
left() Left-censored response: event occurred before the observation time.
multistate() Multi-state or competing-risks response: track which of multiple outcomes occurs.
right() Right-censored response: the standard and most common form of survival data.
to_dict() Return a JSON-ready mapping fully describing the response.
to_frame() Return the response as a DataFrame (one row per observation).
to_json() Serialize to a deterministic JSON string.
### [counting()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Counting-process response: track subjects entering and exiting the risk set at ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L388-L458){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python counting(start, stop, event=None, *, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} different times. The counting-process form handles two important real-world complexities: 1. **Late entry (left truncation)**: Not all subjects start being at risk at time 0. For example, a study might enroll subjects at different ages, or you might analyze a subset of follow-up time after some subjects are already older. The `start` time marks when each subject becomes eligible to experience the event. 2. **Time-varying covariates**: The counting-process form naturally accommodates covariates that change over time. Each row represents one interval of time for a subject, allowing you to track how covariate values change. Each subject contributes one or more (start, stop] intervals. The subject is at risk only during their interval(s) and cannot experience the event before entering at `start`. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [start]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Entry times (when each subject becomes at risk). Must be finite and non-negative. Represents when the subject enters the risk set. In standard studies this is `0`. In studies with late entry, it is the age or time at enrollment. [stop]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Exit times (when follow-up ends). Must be finite, non-negative, and strictly greater than the corresponding `start`. Represents when the subject leaves follow-up (event, censoring, or end of study). [event]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Event indicators (`1` = event occurred, `0` = censored at `stop` time). If `None`, all subjects are treated as having experienced the event. [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Case weights (strictly positive, one per subject). Used to weight subjects differently in survival analysis. Default is `None` (all weights are `1`). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A counting-process `Surv` response object with potential left truncation. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Here we have 3 subjects with different entry times: ::: {#42aeea47 .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Build a counting-process response with different entry times y = gw.Surv.counting(start=[0, 2, 1], stop=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=19} ``` Surv(type=counting, n=3, events=2, truncated) ``` ::: ::: The display shows: - Subject 1: Entered at time 0, exited with an event at time 5 - Subject 2: Entered at time 2 (late entry), exited censored at time 6 - Subject 3: Entered at time 1, experienced an event at time 4 Only subjects 2 and 3 benefit from the late entry handling, but the counting-process form elegantly handles all cases uniformly. This representation is also essential for studies with time-varying covariates, where you create multiple rows per subject as their covariate values change. ### [from_dict()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Rebuild a response from `to_dict` output. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L1042-L1091){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python from_dict(data) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} This is the inverse of `to_dict()`: it reconstructs an equivalent `Surv` object from a dictionary previously created by `to_dict()`. Useful for deserializing stored or transmitted data, or for round-tripping through storage formats. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [data]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [dict[str, Any]]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A dictionary produced by `to_dict()` containing keys `type`, `stop`, `status`, and optional keys for `start`, `lower`, `states`, `weights`. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A new `Surv` object with the same data and structure as the input dictionary. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Rebuild an equivalent response from its dictionary representation. Here we serialize a response and immediately deserialize it: ::: {#1573bff3 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Round-trip through dictionary serialization reconstructed = gw.Surv.from_dict(y.to_dict()) print("Objects equal:", y.to_dict() == reconstructed.to_dict()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Objects equal: True ``` ::: ::: The reconstructed object is equivalent to the original in every way. ### [from_json()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Deserialize from `to_json` output. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L1134-L1171){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python from_json(text) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} This is the inverse of `to_json()`: it reconstructs a `Surv` object from a JSON string previously created by `to_json()`. Useful for loading data from stored files, API responses, or any other JSON source. The reconstructed object is guaranteed to be equivalent to the original. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [text]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A JSON string produced by `to_json()` containing the serialized `Surv` data. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A new `Surv` object restored from the JSON representation. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Deserialize from JSON. Build a response, then round-trip through `to_json()` and back: ::: {#41b4566c .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Round-trip through JSON serialization json_text = y.to_json() restored = gw.Surv.from_json(json_text) print("Round-trip successful:", y.to_json() == restored.to_json()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Round-trip successful: True ``` ::: ::: The restored object is an exact copy of the original `Surv` object. ### [interval()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Interval-censored response: event time is known to lie within a range. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L460-L538){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python interval(lower, upper, *, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Interval censoring occurs when you know the event happened sometime between two observation times, but not exactly when. Common in: - **Medical follow-up**: Disease detection between clinic visits. You might know a patient's disease status at two checkups, but not the exact time of onset. - **Equipment reliability**: Failure detected between inspections. You know failure happened between the last working inspection and the current failed one. - **Longitudinal surveys**: Event reported between survey waves but exact timing unknown. The interval-censored form captures this uncertainty. The event happened somewhere in the interval (lower, upper]. If lower == upper, it's an exact (uncensored) event. Use `numpy.inf` for upper to represent right-censoring, and `0` for lower to represent left-censoring. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [lower]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Interval lower bounds (one per subject). Must be finite and non-negative. Event happened *after* this time (possibly at this time). Set to `0` to mark left-censored subjects (event happened before first observation). [upper]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Interval upper bounds (one per subject). Must be finite, non-negative, and >= `lower`. Event happened *by* this time. Set to `numpy.inf` to mark right-censored subjects (no event observed by end of study). [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Case weights (strictly positive, one per subject). Used to weight subjects differently in survival analysis. Default is `None` (all weights are `1`). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : An interval-censored `Surv` response object. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Here we have 3 subjects with different levels of observation precision: ::: {#1520cb42 .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw import numpy as np # Build an interval-censored response with mixed precision y = gw.Surv.interval(lower=[1, 2, 3], upper=[2, np.inf, 5]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=22} ``` Surv(type=interval, n=3, events=2) ``` ::: ::: The display shows: - Subject 1: Exact event at time 2 (lower == upper) - Subject 2: Right-censored at time 2 (upper = infinity means event never observed) - Subject 3: Interval-censored between times 3 and 5 (event happened somewhere in that window) Interval censoring gives you more information than right censoring alone. Rather than just knowing "no event by time X," you may know "event was definitely before time Y but after time X", which allows for more precise estimation when multiple observations bracket the event. ### [left()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Left-censored response: event occurred before the observation time. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L331-L386){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python left(time, event=None, *, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Left censoring occurs when all you know is that an event happened *before* you observed the subject. For example, an infection that must have occurred before a patient was tested, or a failure that was known to have happened sometime before equipment was inspected. The exact event time is unknown, but you know it was no later than the recorded `time`. This is less common than right censoring, but important in scenarios where you cannot pinpoint when something happened, only that it already had. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [time]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Observation times (the upper bound on when the event occurred). Must be finite and non-negative. Each value represents "the event happened by this time". [event]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Event indicators: - `1` = event occurred before `time` (left-censored) - `0` = subject was event-free at `time` (not censored) If `None`, all subjects are treated as having experienced the event. [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Case weights (strictly positive, one per subject). Used to weight subjects differently in survival analysis. Default is `None` (all weights are `1`). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A left-censored `Surv` response object. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Here we have 3 subjects. Two experienced the event before the recorded time (event=1), and one was event-free at observation (event=0): ::: {#4ba45cd6 .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Build a left-censored response y = gw.Surv.left(time=[5, 6, 4], event=[1, 0, 1]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=23} ``` Surv(type=left, n=3, events=2) ``` ::: ::: The display shows the data structure: - The `<` symbol indicates left-censored observations (event occurred before time) - The `+` symbol indicates subjects who were still event-free at the observation time - The left-censoring type `"left"` is displayed at the top ### [multistate()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Multi-state or competing-risks response: track which of multiple outcomes occurs. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L540-L639){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python multistate(time, event, states, *, start=None, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Real-world studies often involve multiple competing outcomes. A patient in a cancer study might relapse, die from cancer, or die from other causes. Each subject can only experience one outcome, and once it happens, no other outcome is possible. The multi-state framework elegantly handles this by: 1. **Defining possible states**: You specify the labeled outcomes (e.g., "relapse", "death from cancer", "death from other causes") that are mutually exclusive. 2. **Recording which state occurred**: Rather than a simple 0/1 event, you record which specific state the subject transitioned to (or 0 if censored). 3. **Separate risk estimation**: You can estimate the risk of each state independently, accounting for the fact that other states prevent each outcome. This is essential for realistic survival modeling: accounting for competing risks often substantially changes the estimated risk curves compared to treating all non-events identically. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [time]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Event or censoring times (one per subject). Must be finite and non-negative. Represents when the subject experienced an outcome (or was censored). [event]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Event codes indicating which state occurred: - 0 = censored (no event observed) - 1 = transitioned to states[0] (first outcome) - 2 = transitioned to states[1] (second outcome) - ... and so on for each defined state Must be in range [0, len(states)]. [states]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [tuple[str, ...]]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Labels for the possible outcomes. Event codes index into this tuple. Example: states=("relapse", "death") means: - event code 1 → relapse occurred - event code 2 → death occurred Labels are arbitrary strings describing what the transition represents. [start]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [array - like]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Optional entry times (for late entry / left truncation). If provided, each subject is only at risk from `start` until `time`. Default is `None` (all subjects enter at time 0). [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [array - like]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Case weights (strictly positive, one per subject). Used to weight subjects differently in survival analysis. Default is `None` (all weights = 1). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A multi-state / competing-risks `Surv` response object. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Here we have 4 subjects with 2 competing outcomes (relapse and death): ::: {#5f650bf4 .cell execution_count=24} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Build a competing-risks response with two outcomes y = gw.Surv.multistate( time=[5, 6, 7, 8], event=[1, 2, 0, 1], states=("relapse", "death") ) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=24} ``` Surv(type=right, n=4, events=3, states=('relapse', 'death')) ``` ::: ::: The display shows: - Subject 1: Transitioned to "relapse" (event code 1) at time 5 - Subject 2: Transitioned to "death" (event code 2) at time 6 - Subject 3: Censored (event code 0) at time 7 - Subject 4: Transitioned to "relapse" (event code 1) at time 8 You can then estimate the probability of each outcome separately, capturing the full picture: not just "will something happen?" but "which specific outcome is most likely?" This avoids the bias of artificially grouping competing outcomes together. ### [right()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Right-censored response: the standard and most common form of survival data. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L261-L329){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python right(time, event=None, *, weights=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Right censoring is the default in survival analysis. It occurs when follow-up ends before the event happens (a subject is still event-free when we last observed them). This is the most common censoring mechanism in practice: - **Study ends**: A clinical trial concludes while some patients are still healthy - **Loss to follow-up**: A subject drops out, moves away, or stops visiting the clinic - **Administrative censoring**: Follow-up ends at a fixed time regardless of status Right censoring is called "censoring from the right" because we know the event happened *after* the censoring time. We record that a subject was event-free at their last observation but don't know how much longer they could have gone. This simple form assumes all subjects enter follow-up at the same reference time (typically time 0). If subjects enter at different times or follow-up is complex, use `counting()` or `interval()` instead. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [time]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Exit times when follow-up ends (one per subject). Must be finite and non-negative. This is the time of either the event or censoring, whichever came first. [event]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Event indicators: - `1` = event occurred (fully observed) - `0` = censored (event time unknown but > time) If `None`, all subjects are treated as having experienced the event (useful for testing or descriptive purposes). [weights]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Case weights (strictly positive, one per subject). Used to weight subjects differently in survival analysis (e.g., inverse probability weighting). The default is `None` (all weights are `1`). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A right-censored `Surv` response object (the most common type). ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} The most common case: subjects have an exit `time` and an `event` indicator (`1` if event occurred, `0` if censored): ::: {#68e22d63 .cell execution_count=25} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw # Build a right-censored response with two events and two censorings y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=25} ``` Surv(type=right, n=4, events=2) ``` ::: ::: The display shows 4 observations with 2 events and 2 censored observations: - Subjects 1 and 3: Event observed (no marker or `*` depending on visualization) - Subjects 2 and 4: Censored (indicated by a `/` marker, still event-free at times 6 and 9) This is the default input format for nearly all survival analysis methods. Right-censored data is so ubiquitous that "survival data" often refers specifically to right-censored observations. ### [to_dict()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Return a JSON-ready mapping fully describing the response. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L995-L1040){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python to_dict() ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} This method serializes the entire `Surv` object into a plain Python dictionary, making it suitable for JSON serialization, storage, or transmission. All array data is converted to plain Python lists. The dictionary captures the censoring type and every array (time, status, optional fields), with `None` for fields that a given censoring flavor does not use. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [dict[str, Any]]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A dictionary with keys: `type` (CensoringType as string), `stop`, `status`, and optional keys `start`, `lower`, `states`, `weights` (as lists or None). ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} The mapping structure varies by censoring type, but always includes `type`, `stop`, and `status`. Unused fields are `None`. Here we build a response and convert it to a dictionary: ::: {#d6417b99 .cell execution_count=26} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Serialize the response to a plain dictionary y.to_dict() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=26} ``` {'type': 'right', 'stop': [5.0, 6.0, 4.0, 9.0], 'status': [1, 0, 1, 0], 'start': None, 'lower': None, 'states': None, 'weights': None} ``` ::: ::: This is the serialized form that underpins `to_json()` and enables round-tripping via `from_dict()`. ### [to_frame()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Return the response as a DataFrame (one row per observation). ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L945-L993){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python to_frame(*, format=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Exports the `Surv` object to a tidy table where each row represents one observation. The table includes the `stop` and `status` columns, plus optional columns for `start` (entry time in counting-process form), `lower` (lower bound for interval censoring), and `weight` (case weights). This is convenient for inspection, export to CSV, or integration with other DataFrame workflows. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [format]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Output format: `None` (default), `"pandas"`, `"polars"`, or `"pyarrow"`. When `None`, a backend is auto-detected (Polars, then Pandas, then PyArrow). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [pandas.DataFrame, polars.DataFrame, or pyarrow.Table]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A tidy table with one row per observation, including columns for `stop`, `status`, and optional `start`, `lower`, `weight` columns. ::: #### Raises {.doc-raises} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ImportError]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : If the requested (or, when auto-detecting, any) DataFrame library is not installed. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Build a right-censored response and export it as a Polars frame. Each row represents one observation with its event time and status: ::: {#8eaf5deb .cell execution_count=27} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Export the response as a Polars DataFrame y.to_frame(format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=27} ```{=html}
shape: (4, 2)
stopstatus
f64i64
5.01
6.00
4.01
9.00
``` ::: ::: Request a different backend with `format=`: ::: {#ad177b39 .cell execution_count=28} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Export as a Pandas DataFrame instead y.to_frame(format="pandas") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=28} ```{=html}
stop status
0 5.0 1
1 6.0 0
2 4.0 1
3 9.0 0
``` ::: ::: ### [to_json()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Serialize to a deterministic JSON string. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_surv.py#L1093-L1132){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python to_json(*, indent=2) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} This method converts the entire `Surv` object to a compact, JSON-formatted string suitable for storage in files, databases, or transmission over APIs. By default, the output is human-readable with indentation; pass `indent=None` for a compact form. The serialization is deterministic: the same `Surv` object always produces the identical JSON string. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [indent]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[2]{.dv}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Number of spaces to use for indentation. If `None`, produces compact JSON without whitespace. Default is 2 (human-readable). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A JSON string representing the `Surv` object, including censoring type and all arrays. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Serialize to JSON. By default, output is indented for readability. Here we build a response and show just the first 120 characters of compact JSON: ::: {#0690fbde .cell execution_count=29} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw y = gw.Surv.right(time=[5, 6, 4, 9], event=[1, 0, 1, 0]) # Serialize to compact JSON json_compact = y.to_json(indent=None) print(json_compact[:120]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` {"type": "right", "stop": [5.0, 6.0, 4.0, 9.0], "status": [1, 0, 1, 0], "start": null, "lower": null, "states": null, "w ``` ::: ::: The full JSON includes all data in a structured format that can be parsed back with `from_json()`.
